topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, cream-colored, bearing an address I hadn't seen in a decade. My hand trembled slightly as I slid the letter across the mahogany desk. Inside, the script was formal, painstakingly polite, an exercise in contrite phrasing. "Please accept this belated expression of regret for the profound hurt I caused you..." The words swam before my eyes, meaningless artifacts of a past I was desperately trying to compartmentalize. I reread the final paragraph, the signature a familiar, infuriating flourish. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a physical clenching that made my jaw ache. I didn't bother tearing it open immediately. Instead, I placed it on the corner of the blotter, a silent judgment radiating from the paper. I stood up, the movement stiff, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, the weight of that belated acknowledgment pressing down on my chest like a physical burden.

[story 2]

When the postman delivered the package, I barely looked up from my work. It was an unsolicited letter, dated from five years prior. The paper felt cheap, almost flimsy, a stark contrast to the permanence of the transgression it addressed. I picked it up with two fingers, as if handling something contaminated. The content was an elaborate tapestry of apologies, full of flowery language about misjudgment and regret. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I crumpled the letter slowly, deliberately, the sound of the tearing paper echoing too loudly in the quiet study. I watched the scraps fall to the thick Persian rug, my knuckles white where I gripped the remaining pieces. I needed to burn it, to erase the physical evidence of that sentiment, but a strange inertia held me captive. I just stared at the pile, my gaze vacant, unable to move toward the fireplace.

[story 3]

I found the letter tucked inside a forgotten book, the paper yellowed with age. Opening it felt like breaching a sealed vault, releasing something toxic into the air. The apology was comprehensive, detailing every perceived slight, every moment of carelessness that had left a lasting scar. Reading it, a wave of nausea rolled over me, making my stomach churn violently. I dropped the letter onto the floor, the sound sharp and final. I didn't bother to retrieve it. I paced the length of the room, my steps heavy and uneven, each footfall a measure of the internal turmoil the letter had just ignited. My hands clenched into fists, the tension radiating up my arms until my shoulders were rigid. I wanted the sensation of the paper under my fingertips, the tangible proof of the offense, but I couldn't bring myself to touch it again.
